diagnostics.consequence: Because the LLM evaluator chooses which node is carried into later stages, evaluator error or bias can directly shape the experiment trajectory; the packet does not report a calibration or error analysis for that evaluator.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: After each stage, the experiment manager selects the best-performing node using a dedicated LLM evaluator (see next section) based on clearly articulated criteria. This selected node is then carried forward to seed the subsequent experimentation stage. The manager also records checkpoints at each stage’s completion.

New child nodes are created differently depending on their parent node’s status: For non-buggy nodes, refinement is applied to improve the experiment code for better performance. For buggy nodes, the system attempts to debug them using stored error information. A best-performing node, selected by LLM-based evaluation, is passed down as the root node of Stage 2.

At each iteration, the system selects several nodes from the existing tree to expand in parallel. With a predefined probability, a buggy node is chosen (thus prioritizing error resolution and debugging); otherwise, a non-buggy node is selected for further refinement and improvement. When choosing between non-buggy nodes, the system uses a best-first search strategy, guided by an LLM that evaluates candidates based on factors like performance metrics, training dynamics, and the quality of generated plots.

diagnostics.statement: LLM-based evaluation is an implemented decision mechanism that selects nodes for expansion and stage transition, but its reliability is not evidenced in the packet.

diagnostics.consequence: Claims about autonomy can be overstated if human selection of starting ideas and final manuscripts is not separated from autonomous work inside a single run.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: From this combined AI-generated pool, we selected the three most promising initial ideas–two from the first batch, and one from the second batch–based on alignment with the workshop theme and potential interest, focusing on topics representing distinct research directions. This initial idea selection step allowed us to manage computational resources by choosing which distinct, AI-generated starting points to explore further with the full system. It did not involve modifying the ideas themselves.

From the multiple complete manuscripts generated for each initial idea (i.e., one manuscript per seed), we selected the single best-resulting manuscript for submission based on a careful inspection of its overall coherence and scientific quality. This process mimics a professor reviewing the work of many students or teams and deciding which work is ready to be submitted for peer review.

Crucially, while humans initiated the process by providing the high-level workshop theme and selected which initial AI-generated ideas to run multiple times through the full pipeline (akin to deciding which experiments to fund or prioritize), and subsequently selected the most promising complete output from those multiple runs, the entire process within any single run—hypothesis refinement, code generation, execution, analysis, visualization, and writing—was performed autonomously

diagnostics.statement: The packet distinguishes autonomous single-run execution from human-controlled selection of initial ideas and final outputs, creating a boundary ambiguity for system-level autonomy.

diagnostics.consequence: The workshop outcome provides limited evidence of consistent end-to-end manuscript quality across generated submissions.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: Blind Peer Review: The three AI-generated submissions were included among the 43 total submissions received by the ICBINB workshop. Reviewers were informed in advance that some submissions might be AI-generated, but were not told which submissions were produced by The AI Scientist-v2. Reviewers could also opt out of reviewing potentially AI-generated manuscripts.

Among the three manuscripts produced by The AI Scientist-v2, one manuscript achieved a sufficiently high average reviewer score (6.33 out of 10, with individual scores of 6, 6, and 7) to surpass the workshop’s acceptance threshold. The remaining two submissions received lower scores and were not accepted.

diagnostics.statement: Two of three AI-generated workshop submissions were not accepted after blind peer review.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: After each stage, the experiment manager selects the best-performing node using a dedicated LLM evaluator (see next section) based on clearly articulated criteria. This selected node is then carried forward to seed the subsequent experimentation stage. The manager also records checkpoints at each stage’s completion.

New child nodes are created differently depending on their parent node’s status: For non-buggy nodes, refinement is applied to improve the experiment code for better performance. For buggy nodes, the system attempts to debug them using stored error information. A best-performing node, selected by LLM-based evaluation, is passed down as the root node of Stage 2.

At each iteration, the system selects several nodes from the existing tree to expand in parallel. With a predefined probability, a buggy node is chosen (thus prioritizing error resolution and debugging); otherwise, a non-buggy node is selected for further refinement and improvement. When choosing between non-buggy nodes, the system uses a best-first search strategy, guided by an LLM that evaluates candidates based on factors like performance metrics, training dynamics, and the quality of generated plots.

success_check.comparator_or_threshold: Report evaluator agreement, error rate, or calibration against an independently scored node set; threshold should be predefined before evaluation.
success_check.evidence_needed: A held-out set of candidate tree nodes, independent human or external-review labels, evaluator scores, selected nodes, and the computed agreement/calibration/error metrics.
success_check.observable: Measured reliability of the internal LLM evaluator when selecting nodes for expansion or stage transition.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: From this combined AI-generated pool, we selected the three most promising initial ideas–two from the first batch, and one from the second batch–based on alignment with the workshop theme and potential interest, focusing on topics representing distinct research directions. This initial idea selection step allowed us to manage computational resources by choosing which distinct, AI-generated starting points to explore further with the full system. It did not involve modifying the ideas themselves.

From the multiple complete manuscripts generated for each initial idea (i.e., one manuscript per seed), we selected the single best-resulting manuscript for submission based on a careful inspection of its overall coherence and scientific quality. This process mimics a professor reviewing the work of many students or teams and deciding which work is ready to be submitted for peer review.

Crucially, while humans initiated the process by providing the high-level workshop theme and selected which initial AI-generated ideas to run multiple times through the full pipeline (akin to deciding which experiments to fund or prioritize), and subsequently selected the most promising complete output from those multiple runs, the entire process within any single run—hypothesis refinement, code generation, execution, analysis, visualization, and writing—was performed autonomously

success_check.comparator_or_threshold: Every reported result separates at least two categories: human-mediated pre/post-run selection and autonomous within-run execution.
success_check.evidence_needed: A run log or methods table identifying each decision point, decision maker, available alternatives, and whether the decision occurred inside or outside a single autonomous run.
success_check.observable: Boundary clarity for which decisions are autonomous versus human-selected.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: Blind Peer Review: The three AI-generated submissions were included among the 43 total submissions received by the ICBINB workshop. Reviewers were informed in advance that some submissions might be AI-generated, but were not told which submissions were produced by The AI Scientist-v2. Reviewers could also opt out of reviewing potentially AI-generated manuscripts.

Among the three manuscripts produced by The AI Scientist-v2, one manuscript achieved a sufficiently high average reviewer score (6.33 out of 10, with individual scores of 6, 6, and 7) to surpass the workshop’s acceptance threshold. The remaining two submissions received lower scores and were not accepted.

success_check.comparator_or_threshold: Acceptance-rate and reviewer-score confidence intervals are reported over more than three independently generated submissions.
success_check.evidence_needed: Additional independently generated manuscripts, blind-review scores, acceptance decisions, and the same acceptance threshold used for comparison.
success_check.observable: Replicated external-review performance across a larger set of AI-generated submissions.

memo: Implemented evaluation mechanisms in the packet include literature-database novelty checking during idea formulation, LLM-based selection of best-performing experiment-tree nodes, VLM critique of generated visualizations that can mark nodes buggy or non-buggy and preserve feedback, human selection of initial ideas and final manuscripts, and blind workshop peer review of submitted manuscripts. Outputs affect later decisions by determining whether ideas are pursued, which node seeds later stages, whether nodes are debugged or refined, which starting ideas receive full runs, which manuscript is submitted, and whether submissions clear the workshop acceptance threshold. The main architecture risks are unevidenced reliability of internal LLM evaluators, unclear autonomy boundaries around human selection, and limited external-review success across only three submitted manuscripts.
